description:
At extreme high latitudes during the summer solstice, the sun never sets; instead, it traces a full 360-degree circle around the horizon. A standard south-facing solar array will miss the 'nighttime' sun located in the northern sky, losing significant potential energy. This task requires you to design a multi-directional or tracking solar array for an Arctic research station that provides uninterrupted 24-hour power generation while adhering to a strict physical constraint for snow clearance.

Goal: Configure a solar array at 78° N latitude that produces uninterrupted power for all 24 hours of the summer solstice, while maintaining a minimum 30° tilt to shed heavy snow.

1. Open the existing project ~/Documents/Energy3D/polar_base.ng3.
2. Change the geographic location to Latitude 78° N (e.g. near Svalbard).
3. Set the date to June 21st (Summer Solstice).
4. Design a solar array (rooftop or ground) capable of capturing sunlight from all 360 degrees. You may use panels facing multiple cardinal directions or utilize solar trackers.
5. Constraint: Every solar panel must have a tilt angle of at least 30° (if fixed) or utilize dynamic tracking to shed snow.
6. Run the Daily Yield Analysis.
7. Right-click the resulting graph and Export the raw data to a CSV file at ~/Documents/Energy3D/midnight_sun_yield.csv.
8. Save the modified Energy3D project as ~/Documents/Energy3D/polar_base_solved.ng3.
